claims.claim_text: CUDA is NVIDIA's GPU compute platform.
claims.rationale: The official NVIDIA cuda-python repository directly describes CUDA as NVIDIA's platform and runtime surface for Python access.

claims.claim_text: CUDA compatibility depends on using a driver/toolkit combination that NVIDIA documents as compatible.
claims.rationale: NVIDIA's CUDA Compatibility guide is the primary source for this claim, but docs.nvidia.com is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: ROCm is AMD's GPU compute software stack.
claims.rationale: The official ROCm repository README directly defines ROCm as AMD's open-source stack for GPU computation.

claims.claim_text: ROCm official support varies by GPU architecture and operating system combination.
claims.rationale: The official ROCm repository points to a compatibility matrix covering ROCm versions, OS kernels, and GPU architectures, which directly supports the compatibility warning.

claims.claim_text: Framework builds differ by compute target, with distinct CPU-only, CUDA, and ROCm paths.
claims.span_hint: section: Compatibility Checklist That Ages Well | Failure 2: Driver and Framework Mismatch
claims.rationale: The official PyTorch repository documents separate CPU-only, CUDA, and ROCm build paths, which supports the module's framework-build compatibility guidance.

further_reading.title: NVIDIA cuda-python
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for understanding CUDA's platform/runtime surface from the Python side of local AI environments.

further_reading.title: ROCm Official Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for ROCm overview, supported hardware and OS guidance, and links to the official compatibility matrix.

further_reading.title: PyTorch Official Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for seeing how a major framework distinguishes CPU, CUDA, and ROCm installation and build paths.

notes: No claim entries were created for analogies, checklist questions, quiz/exercise content, code blocks, or the pedagogical comparison table.
